project_overview.project_name: Rural Access and Agricultural Marketing Project - Scale Up (RAAMP-SU)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve rural access and climate resilience of communities in targeted rural areas and strengthen institutional capacity for management of the rural road network and in case of an eligible crisis or emergency, respond promptly or effectively to it.
project_overview.total_financing: US$600 million (US$500 million IDA, US$100 million counterpart funding)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 4 million rural beneficiaries including rural farmers, transport operators, market participants, women, children, and rural communities dependent on rural roads. Institutional beneficiaries include federal and state entities managing rural road networks.

primary_locations: All 36 states of Nigeria plus Federal Capital Territory (FCT)
Participating states under ongoing RAAMP: Abia, Bauchi, Katsina, Niger, Ogun, Sokoto (sample roads identified)
Expansion to nationwide coverage subject to state eligibility criteria
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes, partial. Project explicitly targets fragile geographic areas vulnerable to conflict. Northeast (Boko Haram insurgency), Northwest (criminal gangs and banditry), and Northcentral (farmer-herder conflicts, inter-communal wars) regions identified as conflict-affected. Project aims to demonstrate positive state presence in fragile areas. Approximately 3 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) in Nigeria, especially in the North and middle belt.

key_components.component_name: Component A: Improvement of Resilient Rural Access
key_components.description: Rehabilitation/upgrading of approximately 3,000 km of climate-informed rural roads with resilience measures (bridges, culverts, drainage, slope stabilization). Includes supplemental technical support for design, environmental/social safeguards, and supervision. Establishes apprenticeship program within RARAs with focus on gender inclusion (50% women target).
key_components.financing: US$379 million (US$312 million IDA, US$34 million state, US$33 million federal)

key_components.description: Climate-risk informed routine and periodic maintenance of 3,500 km of rural roads. Development and implementation of climate risk-informed road asset management system integrating climate risks module into NiTRIMS. Support for safe access to schools initiative with road safety measures (speed humps, pedestrian crossings, traffic signs).
key_components.financing: US$158 million (US$130 million IDA, US$14 million state, US$14 million federal)

key_components.component_name: Component C: Institutional Strengthening and Project Management
key_components.description: Project management costs, technical assistance, training, and monitoring/evaluation. Support for federal and state-level road sector reforms. Development of climate risk assessment and management frameworks. Road safety institutional strengthening. Support to NADF for sustainable rural road maintenance (US$20 million allocation).
key_components.financing: US$63 million (US$58 million IDA, US$2 million state, US$3 million federal)

key_components.component_name: Component D: Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC)
key_components.description: Allows rapid reallocation of uncommitted IDA financing in event of eligible emergency (natural disasters, climate-related events) as defined in OP 8.00. Guided by CERC Manual and Emergency Action Plan.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (FMAFS) at federal level through newly established National Agriculture Development Fund (NADF) Rural Roads Directorate. State Rural Access Road Agencies (RARAs) at state level with embedded State Project Implementation Units (SPIUs).
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Federal Project Management Unit (FPMU) embedded within NADF coordinates with state-level RARAs. Federal Project Financial Management Department (FPFMD) and state Project Financial Management Units (PFMUs) handle financial management. Annual review of program to calibrate programming based on state eligibility and implementation readiness. Subsidiary Agreements required between federal government and participating states.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (September 2023). Multiyear procurement planning to consolidate activities and avoid small-value transactions. Framework agreements for standard goods. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents for international open competitive procurement. National Procurement Procedures for national open competitive procurement. Electronic Government Procurement (eGP) system used in eligible states. STEP system for tracking procurement transactions. Business sounding events and anti-corruption awareness sessions planned.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Semi-annual unaudited Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) and annual Consolidated Annual Audited Financial Statements (CAAFS). Results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators tracked annually. Use of Geo-Enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) and Mapillary for digital monitoring in difficult-to-access areas. Biannual implementation support missions. Mid-term review at start of year 3. Third-party monitoring for SEA/SH, gender, and grievance mechanisms. Beneficiary satisfaction surveys targeting 80% satisfaction rate.

identified_risks.risk_description: Recent change in administration (May 2023) creates fluid political dynamics. Potential for disruptions in reform implementation or policy reversals. Escalating security concerns could impact specific states. However, new administration has demonstrated commitment to economic reforms including removal of gasoline subsidy and exchange rate policy reforms.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ongoing intensive dialogue with government counterparts. Broad consensus across government tiers on need for reforms. Complementary World Bank projects to address policy reversal risks. Support for medium-term action plans to build on reform momentum.

identified_risks.risk_description: Inflation and currency depreciation risks could impact project costs. Some states have demonstrated difficulties providing counterpart funding for road maintenance under ongoing RAAMP. Failure to contain inflation and currency depreciation may result in volatile project costs.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Inflation and currency impacts considered in project costing and design. Federal counterpart funding commitment. Eligibility criteria require states to demonstrate budget allocation for RARA/SRF operations for at least one year.

identified_risks.risk_description: Inadequate policy framework and resources for maintenance of improved rural infrastructure. Strategies for rural transport sector absent or not well-articulated at state level. Fragmentation in rural asset management between institutions without clear role definitions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports efforts to ensure sustainable rural road asset management and financing at federal and state levels. Expected to ignite policy dialogue and involvement of financial partners. Institutional reforms and capacity building supported through project.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Limited capacity for road safety, road asset management, climate resilience, and implementation of environmental and social standards. Fragmentation in rural asset management without clear ownership at state level. Inadequate funding for rural road development and maintenance.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: RAAMP-SU aims at building institutional capacity at federal and state levels. Institutional reforms and capacity building supported through project. Embedding FPMU within NADF and SPIUs within RARAs ensures sustainability. Technical assistance for new states to establish well-functioning RARAs with qualified staff.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of using financing proceeds for unintended purposes. Possibility of interference in FM staff deployment. Weak capacity of FM staff in preparing IFRs and internal audit reports. Fraud and corruption risks. Poor procurement planning practices and delayed procurement implementation. High number of SPIUs with high volume of procurement activities subject to post-review.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust action plan and implementation support arrangements. Multiyear procurement planning to consolidate activities. Hiring of TA consultants for climate-resilient designs and bidding documents. Business sounding events and anti-corruption awareness sessions. Strengthened internal control mechanisms. Due diligence on bidders' qualifications. HEIS deployment where low capacity identified. FM staff with IDA project experience assigned. Quarterly internal audit reports. Regular FM implementation support missions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Increased security risks in recent years are major constraint on supervision. Conflict events have proliferated, displacing populations and disrupting markets. Boko Haram insurgency in Northeast, criminal gangs and banditry in Northwest, farmer-herder conflicts in Northcentral. Approximately 3 million IDPs in Nigeria.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Implementation of geo-enabling and remote supervision monitoring tools (GEMS and Mapillary) for difficult-to-access and fragile areas. Third-party monitoring emphasized. Security Advisor to be hired no later than three months after effectiveness. State-level Security Risk Management Plans required. GEMS facilitates dissemination of project-level results and promotes community ownership. Project aims to demonstrate positive state presence in fragile areas.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential environmental impacts from civil works including air quality deterioration, dust, vibration, noise pollution, water impacts, occupational health and safety risks. Vegetation clearing along road right-of-way, soil contamination from oil leakage, traffic congestion during construction. Sand mining from burrow pits causing land degradation. Water body pollution from runoff. Hazardous waste generation (asphalt, bitumen). Social risks include involuntary land acquisition, economic displacement, crop losses, livelihood impacts, labor influx, impacts on worship centers.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Plans (ESMPs) prepared for specific sites. Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) prepared as needed. Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) with specific commitments. Labor Management Procedures in place. Chance find procedures included. Project excludes roads through forests, wildlife sanctuaries, national parks, critical habitats. Only vegetation within right-of-way affected. Risks expected to be temporary and site-specific. Borrower has experience from previous RAMP projects.

identified_risks.risk_description: Project roads exposed to climate-related natural hazards including extreme temperature, water scarcity, drought, erosion, floods, landslides. Coastal areas face storm surge and sea level rise. Climate projections indicate increased temperatures (2.9-5.7°C by end of century), heat wave duration increases (8-55 days annually), precipitation variability increases.
identified_risks.risk_rating: Moderate (residual risk rated low after mitigation)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project focuses on rehabilitation and upgrading of rural roads to climate-resilient standards. Climate vulnerability is main criteria for road selection. Climate-informed spot improvements deployed. Soft measures include institutional capacity building, integration of climate risk considerations in road asset management systems, development of climate resilience guidelines and technical standards. Green Roads for Water approach and nature-based solutions applied where possible. Climate risk module integrated into NiTRIMS. Residual climate risk to project outcome rated as low.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential for inadequate technical design of climate-resilient roads. Risk of poor quality environmental and social safeguards instruments. Difficulty in obtaining representative data for road selection before actual implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Supplemental technical support component (A.2) ensures high-quality technical design including climate resilience and nature-based solutions. Consulting services for ESMP and RAP preparation. Road Safety Screening and Appraisal Tool (RSSAT) applied to highest-ranking roads. Multi-criteria analysis (MCA) for road prioritization including climate vulnerability (40% weight), economic (35%), and social (25%) criteria. Economic analysis conducted on sample roads with EIRR of 19.4% and NPV of US$467.46 million.

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes. Project-level grievance mechanism established with multiple channels for complaint submission (in-person, phone, email, letter, ICT-based platforms, social media). Grievance mechanisms at subnational and national levels. Strengthening of existing GM system. World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) available. World Bank Accountability Mechanism (AM) with Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service available. Beneficiary satisfaction surveys conducted annually targeting 80% satisfaction rate.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project addresses gender gaps in physical access to socio-economic infrastructure and employment. Rural roads selected based on potential to increase access to health centers (especially maternal care facilities), schools, and markets. Road designs incorporate gender-responsive safety features including walking infrastructure and proper lighting for personal safety. Apprenticeship program within RARAs targets 50% women participation to increase women's representation in transport sector (currently only 3.43% of transportation sector workforce). Support for women's access to basic services, particularly maternal care in rural areas where maternal mortality is very high (1,047 per 100,000 compared to regional average of 536). Project contributes to World Bank Gender Strategy 2024-2030 on transportation as enabling service.
vulnerable_groups: Rural poor and extremely poor populations
Women (particularly in rural areas with limited access to maternal health services)
Children (over 40% of road traffic disabilities are children under 15; project supports Safe Access to Schools initiative)
Youth (apprenticeship program targets young men and women)
Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - approximately 3 million in Nigeria, especially in North and middle belt
Persons dependent on rural roads for daily economic and social activities

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Targets fragile geographic areas vulnerable to conflict to demonstrate positive state presence and wider stability within fragile context. Aims to reinforce economic and social inclusion of beneficiary communities in conflict-affected areas by increasing access to markets and jobs. Project recognizes that conflict events have proliferated, displacing populations, disrupting markets, and interrupting livelihoods, hampering poverty reduction efforts. Security Risk Management Plan (SRMP) being developed under ongoing RAAMP to address potential security risks before commencement of project interventions. Mitigation measures reflected in contract documents. Project contributes to food security in areas with food shortage, addressing conflict drivers related to resource scarcity.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria faces substantial fragility challenges: (1) Conflict-driven fragility: Boko Haram insurgency in Northeast (since 2009), criminal gangs and banditry in Northwest, farmer-herder conflicts and inter-communal wars in Northcentral. Approximately 3 million IDPs, especially in North and middle belt. (2) Climate-driven fragility: Nigeria ranks 152 out of 187 on Notre Dame GAIN Index indicating high exposure and sensitivity to climate change with low adaptive capacity. Droughts exacerbating land degradation and desertification in north. Floods affecting millions (2012 floods affected 7 million people in 30 states, cost US$17.3 billion or 1.4% of GDP). Climate inaction could cost 6-30% of GDP by 2050. (3) Food security fragility: Over 26.5 million people projected to face acute hunger by August 2024. Agriculture sector highly vulnerable to climate change. Nigeria loses up to 25% of farm produce due to lack of market access. (4) Poverty-driven fragility: Over 40% of Nigeria's population live in poverty, disproportionally rural. Nigeria houses world's second-largest population living in extreme poverty. Overlap between conflict and poverty particularly acute in northern regions. (5) Institutional fragility: Inadequate financing and unclear institutional architecture for rural roads. Fragmentation in rural asset management without clear ownership. Limited capacity for climate resilience and environmental/social standards implementation.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks identified as substantial constraint on supervision. Increased security risks in recent years. Project implements geo-enabling and remote supervision monitoring tools (GEMS and Mapillary) to overcome supervision limitations posed by insecurity in rural areas. Third-party monitoring emphasized for implementation in difficult-to-access and fragile areas. Security Advisor to be hired no later than three months after effectiveness as legal covenant. State-level Security Risk Management Plans required as disbursement condition. GEMS enables tracking of implementation progress and security risks affecting project communities. Project design allows for advance contracting and retroactive financing to enable contract signature and commencement of civil works at effectiveness, reducing implementation delays in insecure environments.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes. Approximately 3 million internally displaced persons (IDPs) in Nigeria as of November 2021, especially in North and middle belt regions. Project areas overlap with conflict-affected regions where displacement is occurring. Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed. Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) to be prepared for specific sites where involuntary land acquisition occurs. Land acquisition expected to be minimal, localized, and limited to setbacks within existing right-of-way. Federal and state implementing entities have proven capacity from previous RAMP projects (RAMP-1, RAMP-2, ongoing RAAMP). Project aims to support resilience and livelihoods of affected populations through improved rural access and market connectivity.
